text:
The stability of extracellular enzyme activity plays a pivotal role in ecosystem health, and is widely regarded as being increased via adsorption onto mineral surfaces within soil and sedimentary environments. While the oxidation of mineral-bound Fe(II) by oxygen produces reactive oxygen species (ROS), it is not well understood how this impacts upon the stability or activity of adsorbed enzymes. Within this study, we have investigated the effect of mineral-bound Fe(II) oxidation upon the activity of $\beta$-glucosidase (BG) an enzyme responsible for hydrolysis at the cellulose cleavage point. Through a comparison of mineral surfaces composed of pre-reduced nontronite and montmorillonite clay minerals versus the iron-oxide surface of magnetite at pH 5 and 7, we demonstrate for the first time the impact that redox conditions can have on the enzyme-mineral interactions.

Adsorption to mineral surfaces under anoxic conditions initially inhibited BG enzyme activity (indicated as immediate BG activity), and served a protective role by ‘sheltering’ the enzyme for a longer period. Upon introduction of oxygen, the minerals produced reactive oxygen species (ROS) where the level of hydroxyl radical production was linear to the degree of structural Fe(II) oxidation. The ROS, specifically the hydroxyl radical, caused the conformational unfolding and structural decomposition of the BG enzyme molecule, resulting in a swift reduction in enzyme activity and a shorter enzyme lifetime. The oxidative inactivation of the enzyme, therefore, in oxic environments overcomes the protective effect provided by mineral adsorption. This study reveals a novel mechanism of enzyme inhibition and has implications for modeling enzyme longevity and the enzyme activity yield in redox-changing environments, where iron-rich minerals can inhibit enzyme function.
